title: Confessions of a former Eva fantard
display_title: Confessions of a former Eva fantard
wikitext:
[]: Confessions of a former Eva fantard             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Fri Dec 10, 2010 4:52 pm NOTE: In this context I'm using the term "fantard" ONLY to refer to myself, not any other Evangelion fans. Because of my previous over-fixation on and almost ludicrous defense of it, I feel the term appropriately describes my previous relationship with Eva. You are (not) guilty by association. So, where to begin? Evangelion has played a considerable role in my life for several years. It has definitely inspired me and generally enhanced my life. Yet recently something snapped in me. Suddenly, most of the common complaints about it that I used to write off as just feeble minds misunderstanding Eva's greatness and Anno's genius started to make sense. Now, I still completely disagree with everyone who says Eva is just a pretentious, incoherent mish-mash of half-baked philosophy and psychology and meaningless religious imagery. Evangelion proves the strength of its character development by subjecting its characters to psychological and philosophical analysis via ideas about the human condition and individual character that channel Freud, Soren Kierkegaard and Jean-Paul Sartre, and this gives the psychological/philosophical aspect of the series a very important purpose. The head-on collision between Judeo-Christian motifs and secular humanist ideas (NERV/SEELE mediate most of the latter; let's not argue about this) provides a fascinating deconstruction of religious tradition comparable to Nietzsche's Thus Spoke Zarathustra, and because this collision manifests itself in the characters themselves instead of just the narrative, it has a legit two-way dynamic of symbolism to it: the religious motifs symbolize the characters and vice versa. The other main criticism of Eva that I don't think holds any water is the idea that the characters' reactions to what's going on around them isn't realistic. Maybe that's the problem: it's too realistic! Just about anyone would react to subjection to such psychological trauma and cosmic horrors similarly to the characters; their perpetual regression back into their miserable shells is realistic... but is it entertaining? That's a flaw I see in Eva: it supplies both the dazzling action one expects from anime and the philosophical/psychological brain-teasers one expects from "deeper" fiction, but once the magic of the former has subsided and the intrigue of the latter has dulled, what is left but a bunch of sad, broken people (the characters) who make very little progression that isn't all but nullified later on? Some kind of climactic redemption is an understandable expectation for a fictional story, and Eva doesn't deliver it. That isn't to Eva's discredit at all: instead it provides a thought-provoking place for the viewer to search his/her mind and soul, and that makes it a unique and inimitable contribution to anime and fiction in general. But the gist of what I'm saying is that someone who doesn't want or need such "liminal space", who seeks a *gasp!* conventional fiction gratification from Evangelion and is disappointed should NOT be written off as "just ignorant" or in denial of Eva's awesomeness as I used to think and assert. Perhaps I can even relate to Eva critics nowadays. In fiction, I want well-developed, deeply sympathetic characters whose stories I can feel engaged by, like the characters in Eva. But I DON'T want the fiction to fuck with me by abandoning any glimpse of positive development in the characters faster than a sitcom with negative continuity. If I want philosophy or psychology, there are plenty of books for me to read for those purposes. Both can be fantastic additions to a story, but I'd prefer such to be undertones instead of overtones, secondary to keeping the storyline strong, coherent and flowing. (Cowboy Bebop did that pretty well, I'd say.) Life somewhat distanced from Eva has been exceptionally happy for me so far. Do I think Eva is ingenious? Yes. Do I think it's one of the most important and worthy of celebration animes ever? Yes. Is it one of my favorite works of fiction ever? Yes! Do I think that it's a perfect work that all thinking men and women could greatly benefit from watching? No! Thanks for reading. Leave comments, maybe expand on or critique what I've said if you wish.

[]: Re: Confessions of a former Eva fantard             Quote Post by Xard » Fri Dec 10, 2010 5:00 pm Born of Lilith wrote: The head-on collision between Judeo-Christian motifs and secular humanist ideas provides a fascinating deconstruction of religious tradition

[]: Re: Confessions of a former Eva fantard             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Fri Dec 10, 2010 5:20 pm Xard wrote: African-American brother you just went full retard. NGE is about as secular humanist as my dog* nor does it have aany interest in "deconstructing religious traditions". Anno is something of a spiritual person in a weird Japanese way (something akin slight mysticism present in Nausicaä's view on life) and in any case he'd have no reason at all to deconstruct judeochristian tradition. It's not relevant to him at all to begin with nor does he have an ideological axe to grind. Overall, cool story bro. edit: *actually I don't have a dog.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Dec 10, 2010 6:10 pm We seriously need to sticky that quote about Evangelion not having any religious meaning.

[]:             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Fri Dec 10, 2010 6:48 pm NAveryW wrote: We seriously need to sticky that quote about Evangelion not having any religious meaning.

[]: Re: Confessions of a former Eva fantard             Quote Post by Allemann » Sat Dec 11, 2010 6:31 am Born of Lilith wrote: Evangelion proves the strength of its character development by subjecting its characters to psychological and philosophical analysis via ideas about the human condition and individual character that channel Freud, Soren Kierkegaard and Jean-Paul Sartre, and this gives the psychological/philosophical aspect of the series a very important purpose.

[]: Re: Confessions of a former Eva fantard             Quote Post by Xard » Sat Dec 11, 2010 7:22 am Allemann wrote: Everyone harps this restlessly, but no one took the time to make an analysis of existential themes in Evangelion and connect them with philosophy, thus proving the claim. Eva is not that deep in philosophy, especially not in existentialism, nor did Anno ever claim he studied philosophy in depth besides some superficial references. The series is about interpersonal relationships, but this doesn't put you immediately into existentialist territory.

[]: Re: Confessions of a former Eva fantard             Quote Post by Allemann » Sat Dec 11, 2010 8:09 am Xard wrote: eva analizing is kinda derp

[]: Re: Confessions of a former Eva fantard             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Dec 11, 2010 10:13 am Born of Lilith wrote: The other main criticism of Eva that I don't think holds any water is the idea that the characters' reactions to what's going on around them isn't realistic. Maybe that's the problem: it's too realistic! Just about anyone would react to subjection to such psychological trauma and cosmic horrors similarly to the characters; their perpetual regression back into their miserable shells is realistic... but is it entertaining?

[]:             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Sat Dec 11, 2010 11:59 am You make a lot of good points, FreakyFilmFan4ever. The realism of the characters in a crazy, implausible world is definitely an important part of Eva's appeal: without that dynamic, it wouldn't be quite as unique, it'd hardly be Eva! Evangelion executed everything it apparently set out to accomplish very well, but since what it set out to be defies conventional expectations for fiction in so many ways, I think it's really perfectly understandable if someone doesn't always enjoy the show, or at all. That's why I question if the psychological and philosophical aspects that basically come to take over the whole show succeed at entertaining and delivering the plot in a coherent, satisfactory way (the former is of course up for debate, but I think most of us are at least a little skeptical about the latter). (Right here I deviate from the topic of the above and elaborate on my personal disillusionment from Eva a bit. Stream of consciousness, I guess. :P) I think the characters should always be a higher priority in a story than the details of the world that surrounds them. Without them, the fictional world and its details are useless! Seeing the characters' individual stories through to the end was a prominent motive for me in following the story along, but Eva doesn't give its characters much resolve except being turned into Angel menstrual fluids or undergoing psychoanalysis in purgatory. Of course that wouldn't be an issue at all if the characters weren't well-developed and sympathetic, but since Eva boasts some of the most likable and engaging anime characters around, this is especially bothersome. I mean, is anyone NOT a little frustrated when the characters who (at least for me) were the show's best selling point are tossed aside in favor of faux-metaphysical gibberish and indecipherable montages that could be music videos for some industrial metal band? I've actually developed somewhat of an aversion to fiction that dedicates a generous portion of its airtime elaborating on its own brand of pseudoscience (read: magic by any other name). I am burnt out on Evangelion, and that's partly because, as I've implied, I'm drawn to fiction mostly for good characters (which Eva has) and a strong backbone of plot that gets its job done coherently (which Eva doesn't have). I'll also admit that I enjoyed Elfen Lied and Death Note more than I did GITS, Lain, Berserk, Noir, RahXephon and Ergo Proxy (not that I really disliked any of those; hell, GITS is one of my favorite animes). In a nutshell, I read stories for stories and nonfiction for philosophy, psychology and science. Since I'm mostly disinterested in analyzing Eva's science/magic/whatever, I guess it might make sense the series had less longevity for me than it did for others. @Allemann: I've read Sartre, Kierkegaard, Nietzsche and plenty other writers associated with existentialism and I'd say that, while Eva's existentialist flair might be a bit paperthin as far as real life philosophy goes, it's pretty fair for a work of fiction. (I hope none of the above comes off as condescending. My frustration is personal and relative; I'm not trying to take it out on any of the fans or even the show itself per se.)

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Sat Dec 11, 2010 12:31 pm Born of Lilith wrote: I've read Sartre, Kierkegaard, Nietzsche and plenty other writers associated with existentialism and I'd say that, while Eva's existentialist flair might be a bit paperthin as far as real life philosophy goes, it's pretty fair for a work of fiction.

[]:             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Sat Dec 11, 2010 12:42 pm You can't expect the same things from straight-up philosophy fiction and standard fiction with a philosophical bent. That's like complaining about a story with lots of sex on the side not being "real pornography".

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Sat Dec 11, 2010 1:37 pm Born of Lilith wrote: You can't expect the same things from straight-up philosophy fiction and standard fiction with a philosophical bent.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sat Dec 11, 2010 1:48 pm Allemann wrote: Evangelion claims not to be standard fiction.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Dec 11, 2010 1:50 pm I can see what it is you're saying, BoL. And to be honest, the closing scene of The End of Evangelion always left me feeling empty. It's not that everything didn't tie in the end, it's just that it didn't feel right. Regardless, it's been my top favorite movie longer than any other film has been to date. I guess I like the emotional roller-coaster ride, and it's ending just keeps me coming back for more because of it's unresolved tone. Anno's techniques are still the greatest I've seen in animated storytelling in a while, and it'd be hard to sway my opinion of the film's quality on a technical scale. But my emotional connection to the film itself might change by next year. Who knows? The New Theatrical Editions seem to be giving us that flip side. But again, I haven't seen 2.0 yet, and this thread probably shouldn't spiral down into that topic either. But most reviews I've heard of it seem to promise a more hopeful look at the series. Which, let's be honest, would be nice. As effective as Anno was in his methods of storytelling, it'd be nice to see a character from Evangelion and not think "Oh, they ultimately are all stranded in a weird state of limbo at the end."

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Sat Dec 11, 2010 3:00 pm I suppose this does warrant some explanation in broader terms, but the majority of the issues people have with Evangelion stem from preconceptions and standard expectations. As it has been noted already, people generally want a resolution to the plot and a happy ending.... and probably for it all to make sense. When people are looking for entertainment, they usually want something which makes them feel good in the end. This is why the many of the most popular movies are comedies and action films, they give the audience exactly what they want. If the film has sadder moments, chances are it won't be in the ending and everyone will be smiling in the end (or occasionally crying tears of joy). Pixar's films hit both ends of the spectrum, but always seem to have a happy ending ( Up! is particularly heavy on the tearjerking, but it starts out sad, but you'll be smiling at the end). Neon Genesis Evangelion is better classified as a tragedy than an adventure or comedy, and this particular detail usually goes unmentioned. The fact the central arc of the series (episodes 7-13, or the "Monster of the Week" segment where they go through an angel every episode.... come to think of it, Rebuild 2.0 fills in this same part of the story) is a healthy mix of action & comedy with exceptional and generally positive character development lulls the uninformed viewer into a false sense of security (a "trap", you could say) for the final parts which may as well be pitch black in comparison. Quite simply, the happy ending they are hoping to find is not coming . And understandably, some viewers do feel a bit betrayed by this. The tragedy elements extend to the characters as well. Tragic heroes (of which ALL the central characters in NGE could be qualified as) tend to more realistic than their idealized counterparts, having clear vices & demons (also known as "flaws") to contend with. Those issues are often their downfall (and quite frequently, their death) within the story, yet the audience still sympathizes with them, hopefully. Shinji's flaw is a bit hard to pin down, you'd think it was his crippling lack of self-confidence.... but it could simply be that he's human and can only take so much before he loses it. Or maybe it's his constant seeking of his father's approval, which he isn't going to get due to his father being afraid to hurt him even more (viscious cycle right there). Asuka's flaw is easier to see, being her superiority complex (which is driven by her own lack of self-esteem she tries to keep hidden). She's an insufferable bitch from hell and knows it, which fuels her self-loathing in the final episodes. Maybe the core flaw of every notable character in Evangelion could simply be a lack of communication, which leads to one form of isolation or another (usually emotional rather than physical). It's a human tale, and it definitely can hit too close to home for some. For those who detest isolation, those "social butterflies", NGE can feel completely foreign and might alienate those viewers. All in all, the focal point of a tragedy is emotional torque . As they say, art is not about making you feel happy. It's about making you feel. Tragedies typically lean towards sad endings because it gets more powerful emotions out of the audience. Unfortunately, in today's day and age tragedy is becoming less and less common. Perhaps it's due to all the instant gratification available out there, people can feel good about themselves by simply looking up a funny video on YouTube. The mystery as to why people want to watch something sad has always been a mystery (catharsis seems to be best explanation), but it's naturally something people will generally avoid. It's easy to understand why many people dislike Evangelion , though depending on your viewpoint it can be either human nature or simple ignorance. Can you really fault something for being a tragedy when you didn't expect it to be? Not really, the impassioned negativity NGE provokes from some is completely unnecessary in my opinion (but as noted, the fanbase usually fails to point this out, fan dumb and hate dumb are two sides of the same coin -- ignorance). Can you say that it's not something you enjoyed because tragedy is not your cup of tea? Yes. I suppose the challenge is getting a sane, intelligent, and respectful opinion (be it for or against) out of the internet. It brings out everyone's inner jerkass for some reason.

[]:             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Sat Dec 11, 2010 3:36 pm The Abhorrent wrote: Can you say that it's not something you enjoyed because tragedy is not your cup of tea? Yes.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Dec 14, 2010 12:35 pm NGE is not a tragedy. Instead it's ambiguous, which means you can read it in lots of different ways. This is a good thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Wed Dec 15, 2010 3:38 pm I found an interesting (archived) Eva review/analysis that addresses the lack of fluid story that I've talked about in this thread. Let's not debate the philosophy parallels, mkay? Otherwise, talk about it. http://web.archive.org/web/20021224003127/http://www.animedorks.com/issues/10/evae.html

[]:             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Wed Dec 15, 2010 5:11 pm The only thing I disagree with is that notion that it's "wrong to estimate or criticize Evangelion as a story." Now, if he's talking about story as if it must be organized to a certain structure or as a story where certain events must happen, then he's right that there's no point to confine Evangelion to a superficial surface to appeal to people who are not interested in the subject in the slightest or make up events are not needed to grow or develop the characters. But as if to not think about Evangelion as a story of the charcter's self growth is just ignoring what the problems are actually there that many people want to overlook. Not saying that's bad or anything, but the more fans overlook the problems that persist in a show like "Evangelion", the more likely they're going to be influenced by frowned-upon practices rather what actually worked and be influenced by those ideas. In other words, there are problems in "Evangelion", and the more we think of them as a illusion or an unneeded element to the franchise (like what Manabu and other users are doing), the worse we'll become as a fanbase.

Because of my previous over-fixation on and almost ludicrous defense of it, I feel the term appropriately describes my previous relationship with Eva. You are (not) guilty by association. So, where to begin?

Article: Confessions of a former Eva fantard
 the philosophical/psychological brain-teasers one expects from "deeper" fiction, but once the magic of the former has subsided and the intrigue of the latter has dulled, what is left but a bunch of sad, broken people (the characters) who make very little progression that isn't all but nullified later on? As effective as Anno was in his methods of storytelling, it'd be nice to see a character from Evangelion and not think "Oh, they ultimately are all stranded in a weird state of limbo at the end."

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Sat Dec 11, 2010 3:00 pm I suppose this does warrant some explanation in broader terms, but the majority of the issues people have with Evangelion stem from preconceptions and standard expectations. As it has been noted already, people generally want a resolution to the plot and a happy ending.... and probably for it all to make sense. When people are looking for entertainment, they usually want something which makes them feel good in the end. This is why the many of the most popular movies are comedies and action films, they give the audience exactly what they want. If the film has sadder moments, chances are it won't be in the ending and everyone will be smiling in the end (or occasionally crying tears of joy). Pixar's films hit both ends of the spectrum, but always seem to have a happy ending ( Up! is particularly heavy on the tearjerking, but it starts out sad, but you'll be smiling at the end). Neon Genesis Evangelion is better classified as a tragedy than an adventure or comedy, and this particular detail usually goes unmentioned. The fact the central arc of the series (episodes 7-13, or the "Monster of the Week" segment where they go through an angel every episode.... come to think of it, Rebuild 2.0 fills in this same part of the story) is a healthy mix of action & comedy with exceptional and generally positive character development lulls the uninformed viewer into a false sense of security (a "trap", you could say) for the final parts which may as well be pitch black in comparison. Quite simply, the happy ending they are hoping to find is not coming . And understandably, some viewers do feel a bit betrayed by this. The tragedy elements extend to the characters as well. Tragic heroes (of which ALL the central characters in NGE

Article: Confessions of a former Eva fantard
 simply, the happy ending they are hoping to find is not coming . And understandably, some viewers do feel a bit betrayed by this. The tragedy elements extend to the characters as well. Tragic heroes (of which ALL the central characters in NGE could be qualified as) tend to more realistic than their idealized counterparts, having clear vices & demons (also known as "flaws") to contend with. Those issues are often their downfall (and quite frequently, their death) within the story, yet the audience still sympathizes with them, hopefully. Shinji's flaw is a bit hard to pin down, you'd think it was his crippling lack of self-confidence.... but it could simply be that he's human and can only take so much before he loses it. Or maybe it's his constant seeking of his father's approval, which he isn't going to get due to his father being afraid to hurt him even more (viscious cycle right there). Asuka's flaw is easier to see, being her superiority complex (which is driven by her own lack of self-esteem she tries to keep hidden). She's an insufferable bitch from hell and knows it, which fuels her self-loathing in the final episodes. Maybe the core flaw of every notable character in Evangelion could simply be a lack of communication, which leads to one form of isolation or another (usually emotional rather than physical). It's a human tale, and it definitely can hit too close to home for some. For those who detest isolation, those "social butterflies", NGE can feel completely foreign and might alienate those viewers. All in all, the focal point of a tragedy is emotional torque . As they say, art is not about making you feel happy. It's about making you feel. Tragedies typically lean towards sad endings because it gets more powerful emotions out of the audience. Unfortunately, in today's day and age tragedy is becoming less and less common. Perhaps it's due to all the instant gratification available out there, people can feel good about themselves by simply looking up a funny video on YouTube. The mystery as to why people want to watch something sad has always been a mystery (catharsis seems to be best explanation), but it's naturally something people will generally avoid. It's easy to understand why many people dislike Evangelion , though depending on your viewpoint it can be either human nature or simple ignorance. Can you really fault something for being a tragedy when you didn't expect it to be? Not really, the

Article: Confessions of a former Eva fantard
. It's easy to understand why many people dislike Evangelion , though depending on your viewpoint it can be either human nature or simple ignorance. Can you really fault something for being a tragedy when you didn't expect it to be? Not really, the impassioned negativity NGE provokes from some is completely unnecessary in my opinion (but as noted, the fanbase usually fails to point this out, fan dumb and hate dumb are two sides of the same coin -- ignorance). Can you say that it's not something you enjoyed because tragedy is not your cup of tea? Yes. I suppose the challenge is getting a sane, intelligent, and respectful opinion (be it for or against) out of the internet. It brings out everyone's inner jerkass for some reason.

Article: Confessions of a former Eva fantard
 to people who are not interested in the subject in the slightest or make up events are not needed to grow or develop the characters. But as if to not think about Evangelion as a story of the charcter's self growth is just ignoring what the problems are actually there that many people want to overlook. Not saying that's bad or anything, but the more fans overlook the problems that persist in a show like "Evangelion", the more likely they're going to be influenced by frowned-upon practices rather what actually worked and be influenced by those ideas. In other words, there are problems in "Evangelion", and the more we think of them as a illusion or an unneeded element to the franchise (like what Manabu and other users are doing), the worse we'll become as a fanbase.
